prompt: My Go web app sometimes returns a 200 OK with truncated HTML when a template has a bug. How do I fix this?
expected: Skill identifies the partial-200 failure and prescribes the two-stage render: execute the template into a bytes.Buffer first; on error call serverError(w, r, err) while w is still clean; only on success call w.WriteHeader(status) then buf.WriteTo(w).

prompt: Why can't I just call ts.Execute(w, data) in my Go handler and then handle the error after?
expected: Skill explains that http.ResponseWriter is a streaming writer: template execution writes bytes (and an implicit 200) incrementally, so once headers are sent a mid-execution error cannot be turned into a 500; buffer to a bytes.Buffer before touching w.

prompt: What is the two-stage render pattern in Go HTTP handlers and when should I use it?
expected: Skill describes rendering the template into an in-memory bytes.Buffer first, then writing the verified buffer to http.ResponseWriter only on success (WriteHeader then WriteTo); use it whenever serving server-rendered HTML to avoid partial-200 corrupted responses.

prompt: When does http.ResponseWriter implicitly send a 200 OK status in Go?
expected: Skill explains the first call to Write() (or explicit WriteHeader) flushes headers including the status, defaulting to 200 OK if WriteHeader was not called; since template execution writes incrementally, this happens mid-render, which is why buffering is required.

prompt: Should I parse Go HTML templates on every request or build a cache at startup? What are the tradeoffs?
expected: Skill recommends building a map[string]*template.Template cache at startup via newTemplateCache(): parse-per-request is slow and surfaces parse errors at runtime, whereas a startup cache is warm from the first request and turns malformed templates into a fail-fast startup error.

prompt: How do I make template parse errors fail at startup instead of surfacing on the first user request?
expected: Skill directs calling newTemplateCache() in main() and, if it returns an error, logging it and os.Exit(1); pre-parsing all pages at startup makes a malformed template a deployment/startup failure rather than a user-visible runtime error.

prompt: Write me a safe render() helper for a Go net/http handler that prevents partial 200 responses on template errors.
expected: Skill produces a render() that looks up the page in app.templateCache (serverError if missing), allocates buf := new(bytes.Buffer), calls ts.ExecuteTemplate(buf, "base", data), returns via serverError on error, and only on success calls w.WriteHeader(status) then buf.WriteTo(w).

prompt: How do I support template hot-reloading in development while using a startup cache in production?
expected: Skill directs gating on an environment/debug flag: in development skip the cache and call newTemplateCache() (rebuild templates) on each request, while production uses the startup-built cache; the two-stage buffered render is unchanged either way.
